camera.title: Set up your camera

checks.positionBad: Step back so full body is visible

camera.guide: Stand 1.5–2 m from camera · Full body in frame

squat.name: Squat
squat.description: Fundamental lower body movement for strength and muscle mass
steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out
Brace your core and keep your chest tall
Push hips back and bend knees until thighs are parallel to the floor
Keep knees tracking over toes — don't let them cave inward
Drive through your heels to stand back up

pushup.name: Push-Up
pushup.description: Upper body pushing movement for chest, shoulders and triceps
steps: Place hands just outside shoulder-width, fingers pointing forward
Extend legs back — body forms a straight line from head to heels
Brace your core and squeeze your glutes before each rep
Lower chest toward the floor, elbows at 45° from your torso
Push the floor away to fully extend your arms

plank.name: Plank
plank.description: Core stability hold targeting the entire midsection
steps: Place forearms on the ground, elbows directly under shoulders
Extend legs fully behind you and raise your hips off the floor
Your body should form a straight line from head to heels
Brace your core, squeeze your glutes — keep hips level with shoulders
Hold without letting hips sag or pike up

lunge.name: Lunge
lunge.description: Unilateral lower body exercise for legs and glutes
steps: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart
Step one foot forward about two feet
Lower your back knee toward the floor — front shin stays vertical
Push through your front heel to return to standing
Alternate legs each rep or complete all reps on one side

shoulderPress.name: Shoulder Press
shoulderPress.description: Overhead pressing movement for shoulder strength
steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, core braced
Hold weights at shoulder height, elbows at 90°, palms facing forward
Press the weights directly overhead until arms are fully extended
Avoid arching your lower back — keep your ribs down
Lower weights back to shoulder height with control

bicepCurl.name: Bicep Curl
bicepCurl.description: Isolation movement for bicep strength and definition
steps: Stand holding weights at your sides, palms facing forward
Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout
Curl the weights up toward your shoulders by bending at the elbows
Squeeze your biceps at the top — hold for a moment
Lower slowly back to starting position

gluteBridge.name: Glute Bridge
gluteBridge.description: Posterior chain exercise targeting glutes and hamstrings
steps: Lie on your back, knees bent, feet flat on the floor hip-width apart
Place arms at your sides, palms facing down
Squeeze your glutes and drive your hips toward the ceiling
Your body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees at the top
Lower your hips with control and repeat

mountainClimber.name: Mountain Climber
mountainClimber.description: Dynamic full-body cardio and core exercise
steps: Start in a high plank with hands under shoulders, body straight
Brace your core and keep hips level with shoulders
Drive one knee toward your chest
Quickly switch legs, driving the other knee in
Maintain a steady rhythm — don't let hips rise

errors.pace: Moving too slowly to get cardio benefit

deadlift.name: Deadlift
steps: Stand with feet hip-width apart, a slight bend in the knees
Hinge at the hips — push them back as you lower the weight
Keep your back flat and chest tall — do not round your spine
Once the weight reaches mid-shin, squeeze glutes to stand up
Lock hips out at the top — do not hyperextend

jumpingJack.name: Jumping Jack
steps: Stand with feet together and arms at your sides
Jump and simultaneously spread your feet to shoulder-width
Raise your arms overhead in an arc as you jump out
Jump back to starting position, bringing feet together and arms down
Land softly on the balls of your feet

tricepDip.name: Tricep Dip
tricepDip.description: Bodyweight pushing movement targeting the triceps using a chair or bench
steps: Sit on the edge of a chair, hands gripping the edge beside your hips
Slide your hips off the chair with legs extended in front of you
Bend your elbows to lower your body toward the floor
Stop when upper arms are parallel to the floor — don't go lower
Push through your palms to fully extend your arms

errors.flare: Elbows flaring out to the sides
errors.depth: Not dipping deep enough — upper arm should reach parallel
errors.shrug: Shoulders shrugging up during the movement

calfRaise.name: Calf Raise
calfRaise.description: Isolation movement for the gastrocnemius and soleus muscles
steps: Stand with feet hip-width apart, toes pointing forward
Hold a wall or chair for balance if needed
Rise onto the balls of your feet as high as possible
Pause at the top and squeeze your calves
Lower your heels slowly — do not bounce at the bottom

errors.knee: Bending the knees — keep legs straight
errors.bounce: Bouncing at the bottom — control the descent

sideLunge.name: Side Lunge
sideLunge.description: Lateral lower body movement targeting inner thighs and glutes
steps: Stand with feet together, core braced
Take a wide step to one side
Bend that knee and push hips back — keep the other leg straight
Lower until your thigh is parallel to the floor
Push through your bent-leg heel to return to center

errors.knee: Knee caving inward over the foot
errors.depth: Not stepping wide enough to get full range

mods.harder: Side lunge with touch or dumbbell

superman.name: Superman
superman.description: Prone back extension to strengthen the erector spinae and glutes
steps: Lie face down, arms extended overhead, legs together
Simultaneously lift your arms, chest, and legs off the floor
Squeeze your glutes and lower back muscles at the top
Hold the top position for 1–2 seconds
Lower everything back to the floor with control

errors.neck: Straining the neck — keep it neutral
errors.hold: Not pausing at the top for 1–2 seconds
errors.range: Not lifting high enough off the floor

mods.harder: Hold a light plate or add resistance band

burpee.name: Burpee
burpee.description: High-intensity full-body movement combining a squat, plank and jump
steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart
Squat down and place hands on the floor in front of you
Jump or step feet back into a high plank position
Perform a push-up (optional), then jump or step feet forward to your hands
Explosively jump up, reaching arms fully overhead

errors.hips: Hips sagging in the plank position
errors.jump: Not fully extending at the jump — arms overhead

mods.easier: Step back instead of jumping — no jump at top
mods.harder: Add a push-up in the plank position

highKnees.name: High Knees
highKnees.description: Running in place with exaggerated knee drive for cardio and core
steps: Stand with feet hip-width apart, arms bent at 90°
Drive your right knee up to hip height while pumping your left arm forward
Quickly switch — drive left knee up, right arm forward
Keep your torso upright — don't lean back
Maintain a fast, rhythmic pace

errors.torso: Leaning back — keep core tight and chest up
errors.pace: Shuffling instead of driving knees up

mods.easier: March in place with high knees
mods.harder: Sprint pace or add arm drive

lateralRaise.name: Lateral Raise
lateralRaise.description: Shoulder isolation movement targeting the medial deltoid
steps: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, weights at your sides
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Raise both arms out to the sides until they reach shoulder height
Keep shoulders down — don't shrug
Lower arms slowly back to your sides

errors.height: Arms not reaching shoulder height at the top
errors.shrug: Traps taking over — shrugging shoulders up
errors.swing: Using momentum to swing the weights up

mods.easier: Cable or resistance band lateral raise

tricepExtension.name: Tricep Extension
tricepExtension.description: Overhead isolation movement for full tricep length under stretch
steps: Stand holding a weight overhead with both hands, arms fully extended
Brace your core and keep elbows close to your head
Slowly lower the weight behind your head by bending at the elbows
Lower until forearms are parallel to the floor — full stretch
Extend arms back up, squeezing your triceps at the top

errors.elbow: Elbows flaring out — keep them close to the head
errors.range: Not lowering fully — full stretch at the bottom
errors.arch: Lower back arching — keep core braced

reverseLunge.name: Reverse Lunge
reverseLunge.description: Backward stepping lunge with less knee stress than forward lunge
steps: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart
Step one foot backward about two feet
Bend both knees to 90° — back knee hovers just above the floor
Keep your front shin vertical and chest upright
Push through your front heel to return to standing

errors.balance: Losing balance — use wall support if needed

wallSit.name: Wall Sit
wallSit.description: Isometric quad hold against the wall — builds endurance and strength
steps: Stand with your back flat against a wall, feet shoulder-width apart
Slide down until your thighs are parallel to the floor
Keep your back pressed against the wall at all times
Rest your hands on your thighs — don't push off with them
Hold for the prescribed time, breathing steadily

errors.angle: Thighs not parallel to the floor — slide down more

mods.easier: Partial wall sit at higher angle
mods.harder: Add weight on thighs or single-leg

squat.depth: Go deeper — aim for 90° knee bend
squat.back: Keep your back straight, chest up

pushup.elbow: Control elbow angle on the way down
pushup.body: Keep body in a straight line

plank.alignment: Align hips with shoulders and ankles

lunge.knee: Keep front knee above ankle, not past toes

press.lockout: Extend arms fully at the top

curl.top: Curl all the way up — squeeze at top

deadlift.hinge: Hinge at hips, keep back neutral

tricepDip.extension: Fully straighten arms at the top

sideLunge.knee: Push knee out over pinky toe — don't let it cave

superman.extension: Lift higher — squeeze glutes at the top

burpee.squat: Squat deep before kicking legs back
burpee.body: Keep hips level in the plank — don't sag

highKnees.lift: Drive knee up to hip height

lateralRaise.height: Raise arms to shoulder level — slightly higher

tricepExtension.elbow: Keep elbows close to your head
tricepExtension.extension: Fully extend arms overhead — squeeze triceps

reverseLunge.knee: Keep front knee over ankle, not past toes

wallSit.angle: Slide down until thighs are parallel to floor
wallSit.back: Press your back flat against the wall

workout.positionYourself: Stand in frame — full body visible
workout.getInPosition: Get in position and press Start

message.good: Good work — small tweaks will perfect it.
message.ok: Decent effort — focus on form next time.
message.needsWork: Slow down and focus on form — quality over speed.

problem.title: 73% exercise with wrong form
problem.description: Bad technique causes injuries, stalls progress, and kills motivation. A personal trainer costs $50–150 per session — out of reach for most. Existing apps show videos but never tell you if you're doing it right.

features.title: Everything you need to train with perfect form

ai.title: Real-Time AI Analysis

1.title: Set Up Your Camera
1.description: Point your phone or laptop camera at your workout space. The AI guides you to the optimal position and checks lighting conditions automatically.

2.title: AI Analyzes Your Form
2.description: 33 body keypoints tracked every frame. Joint angles calculated in real-time and compared against perfect-form reference models for each exercise.

3.title: Get Instant Corrections
3.description: Corrections arrive in under 150ms — text prompts on screen, skeleton overlay, and sound alerts. Fix bad habits before they become injuries.

testimonials.title: Trusted by athletes at every level

1.name: Sarah M.
1.text: I trained at home for 3 years but never knew my squat form was off until AI Coach showed me. Fixed my chronic knee issue in just 2 weeks.

2.name: Alex K.
2.text: As a total beginner I was terrified of injuring myself. AI Coach gave me the confidence to push harder knowing every rep is being checked.

3.name: Maria L.
3.text: The technique score gamification is genius. I went from 72 to 91 average in 6 weeks just trying to beat my own score. Results followed naturally.

1.q: Does my video get sent to your servers?
1.a: No. All AI processing happens entirely on your device using WebAssembly. Your camera feed never leaves your phone or computer — only anonymous metadata (joint angles, scores, rep counts) is synced.

2.q: What devices does it work on?
2.a: iPhone 11+, Android mid-range 2022+ (Snapdragon 665+), and any desktop with Chrome, Edge, or Firefox with a camera and WebGL 2.0 support.

3.q: How accurate is the AI form analysis?
3.a: Joint angles are calculated to ±3° accuracy. Reference models were built with certified personal trainers. The system delivers 25+ FPS on mobile with under 150ms correction latency.

4.q: Can I use it without a dedicated camera setup?
4.a: Yes — any built-in front or rear camera works. The app alerts you if lighting or positioning are suboptimal. A phone stand or tripod is recommended for best results.

5.a: English, Russian, and Kyrgyz are available now. Spanish, German, and Portuguese are planned for the next phase.

learn.title: Learn
learn.subtitle: Video lessons and articles on technique, training, and recovery

formOverReps.title: Form Over Reps — Why Technique Is Everything
formOverReps.summary: Why correct form matters more than rep count, and how sloppy reps wire bad movement patterns into your nervous system.
formOverReps.p1: Most beginners focus on how many reps they can complete, but this misses the point entirely. Every repetition performed with poor form reinforces bad movement patterns in your nervous system. Over time, these patterns become automatic — and automatic bad habits lead to chronic pain and injury.
formOverReps.p2: The neuromuscular system learns by repetition. When you practice a movement correctly, you build efficient neural pathways that improve strength, coordination, and injury resilience. When you rush through sloppy reps, you are literally training yourself to move poorly.
formOverReps.p3: Start lighter. Move slower. Master the pattern before adding load or speed. The AI Fitness Coach tracks your joint angles in real time so you always know when your form breaks down — not after the workout, but during the rep where it matters.

squatForm.title: The Perfect Squat: A Complete Guide
squatForm.summary: Step-by-step breakdown of squat technique: foot position, knee tracking, neutral spine, and the most common errors to avoid.
squatForm.p1: The squat is the king of lower-body movements, but it's also one of the most commonly butchered exercises. The foundation starts with foot position: stand hip to shoulder-width apart, toes pointed out 15–30 degrees. This allows the femur to track over the foot without collapsing inward.
squatForm.p2: On the way down, push your knees out in the direction of your toes and sit back into your hips — don't just bend forward. Your chest should stay tall, spine neutral. The moment your lower back rounds, you have reached your current mobility limit. Stop there and work on hip flexor and ankle mobility to go deeper safely.
squatForm.p3: Depth matters, but not at the expense of spinal alignment. Parallel (thighs parallel to the floor) is a solid target for most people. Below parallel is fine if you can maintain a neutral spine.
squatForm.p4: Common errors: knees caving inward (weak glutes), forward lean (tight hips or ankles), rising on the heels (ankle mobility issue). The AI module tracks your hip, knee, and ankle angles in real time so corrections happen immediately.

pushupProgression.title: Build Your Push-Up from Zero to Advanced
pushupProgression.summary: Correct push-up technique, body alignment, and a step-by-step progression plan for every fitness level.
pushupProgression.p1: The push-up is a fundamental upper-body pressing movement, but very few people perform it correctly. The hands should be just outside shoulder width, fingers pointing forward or slightly out. Your elbows should travel at roughly 45 degrees from your torso — not flared wide (shoulder stress) and not fully tucked.
pushupProgression.p2: The key is body alignment. From head to heels, you should form one rigid plank. Hips that sag down overload the lower back; hips that pike up disconnect the core. Brace your abs and glutes before every single rep.
pushupProgression.p3: Progression path: Wall push-ups → Incline push-ups → Knee push-ups → Full push-ups → Deficit push-ups → Archer push-ups. Move to the next step when you can complete 3 sets of 12 with perfect form.

hipHinge.title: The Hip Hinge: The Movement Pattern That Protects Your Back
hipHinge.summary: What the hip hinge is, why it's critical for safe deadlifts and bends, and how to learn it correctly.
hipHinge.p1: The hip hinge is the foundational movement for deadlifts, Romanian deadlifts, and kettlebell swings. It's also the movement pattern most often missing in people with low back pain — because they flex their spine instead of hinging at the hip.
hipHinge.p2: To learn it: stand 6 inches from a wall. Push your hips back to touch the wall, keeping your knees soft, back flat, and shins nearly vertical. This is the hinge. The movement should feel like you are folding at the crease of your hips, not bending at the waist.
hipHinge.p3: The cue that changes everything: 'proud chest.' When you try to show your chest to the wall in front of you as you hinge, your thoracic spine automatically extends, your lats engage, and your lower back stops rounding. Combine this with pushing the floor away on the way up.

progressiveOverload.title: Progressive Overload: The Only Principle That Drives Gains
progressiveOverload.summary: How and why progressively increasing training demand is the single most important concept for continued progress.
progressiveOverload.p1: Your body adapts to the demands placed on it. When you stop challenging your body beyond its current capacity, adaptation stops. This is progressive overload — the single most important concept in exercise science.
progressiveOverload.p2: Overload does not mean adding weight every session. You can progressively overload by adding reps, adding sets, reducing rest time, slowing the eccentric phase, or improving technique quality. A technique score rising from 70% to 90% is progressive overload.
progressiveOverload.p3: A common mistake is trying to overload too fast. For most people, aim to increase training load by 2.5–5% per week. Track your sessions, scores, and rep counts. The platform records all of this automatically so you can see your trend over time.

setsAndReps.title: Sets and Reps: How to Structure Your Training
setsAndReps.summary: How rep ranges drive different adaptations, how to choose set counts, and why weekly volume matters more than single-session intensity.
setsAndReps.p1: Rep ranges drive different adaptations. 1–5 reps builds maximal strength. 6–12 reps builds hypertrophy (muscle size). 12–20+ reps builds muscular endurance. For general fitness, 8–12 reps per set across 3–4 sets is a practical starting point.
setsAndReps.p2: Rest periods matter as much as rep ranges. Short rests (30–60 seconds) maximize metabolic stress for endurance and fat loss. Longer rests (2–5 minutes) allow fuller recovery for strength work. For hypertrophy, 60–90 seconds is the sweet spot.
setsAndReps.p3: Weekly volume (total sets per muscle group) is what drives growth, not single-workout intensity. Research suggests 10–20 sets per muscle per week for intermediate lifters, distributed across 2–3 sessions. This is exactly how the programs in AI Fitness Coach are structured.

warmupCooldown.title: Why Your Warm-Up and Cool-Down Actually Matter
warmupCooldown.summary: The science behind warming up, what a proper warm-up includes, and why the cool-down is not just tradition.
warmupCooldown.p1: A proper warm-up raises core body temperature, increases blood flow to working muscles, and rehearses the movement patterns you are about to perform under load. Skipping it significantly increases injury risk and reduces performance.
warmupCooldown.p2: An effective warm-up takes 5–10 minutes: 3–5 minutes of light cardio to raise temperature, then dynamic mobility work targeting the joints you will use, then 1–2 warm-up sets of the first exercise at 40–60% of working weight.
warmupCooldown.p3: The cool-down transitions your nervous system from high-performance to recovery mode. 5 minutes of slow walking followed by static stretches held 30–60 seconds on the muscles you worked. This is not just comfort — it improves next-session readiness.

restDays.title: Rest Days Are Not Optional — They Are Where You Grow
restDays.summary: How supercompensation works, why muscle growth happens during recovery not training, and how to spot signs of under-recovery.
restDays.p1: Muscle growth does not happen during a workout. It happens during recovery. When you train, you create micro-tears in muscle fibers and deplete glycogen stores. Rest days are when your body rebuilds those fibers stronger — this is called supercompensation.
restDays.p2: Beginners need more recovery (48–72 hours between training the same muscle). Signs you need more rest: persistent soreness, declining performance, disrupted sleep, elevated resting heart rate, and loss of motivation.
restDays.p3: Rest does not mean sedentary. Active recovery — a 20-minute walk, light yoga, swimming — enhances recovery by increasing blood flow to sore muscles without adding training stress. The programs in AI Fitness Coach include planned rest and active recovery days for exactly this reason.

managingDoms.title: DOMS: What It Is and How to Manage It
managingDoms.summary: What delayed onset muscle soreness actually is, what causes it, what helps, and why soreness is not a reliable indicator of workout quality.
managingDoms.p1: DOMS (Delayed Onset Muscle Soreness) is the stiffness and tenderness you feel 24–72 hours after unfamiliar or intense exercise. It is caused by micro-damage and inflammation in muscle tissue — not lactic acid buildup, which is the old myth.
managingDoms.p2: What helps: light movement (increases blood flow), hydration, adequate protein intake (1.6–2.2 g/kg body weight), contrast showers, and sleep. What does not help: complete rest or pushing through extreme soreness with another heavy session.
managingDoms.p3: DOMS is not a good indicator of training quality. You can have an effective workout without being sore, and you can be very sore from a mediocre workout. As you adapt to training, DOMS becomes less frequent — this is a sign of progress, not stagnation.

sleepMuscle.title: Sleep and Muscle Growth: The Missing Recovery Tool
sleepMuscle.summary: Why sleep is the most underrated performance tool, how sleep deprivation tanks hormones and strength, and how to improve sleep quality.
sleepMuscle.p1: 95% of growth hormone is secreted during deep sleep. Growth hormone is the primary driver of muscle protein synthesis, fat metabolism, and tissue repair after training. If you sleep 5–6 hours per night, you are shortchanging your recovery regardless of how perfect your nutrition and training are.
sleepMuscle.p2: Aim for 7–9 hours of sleep per night on training days. Sleep quality matters as much as quantity: consistent bedtime, a cool dark room, no screens 60 minutes before bed, and minimal caffeine after 2 pm dramatically improve sleep quality.
sleepMuscle.p3: Research shows that sleeping less than 6 hours per night reduces testosterone by 10–15%, increases cortisol (a muscle-wasting hormone), and reduces coordination — all critical for quality training. Prioritizing sleep is one of the highest-return investments you can make in your fitness progress.

startingFitness.title: Starting Your Fitness Journey: A Beginner's Complete Roadmap
startingFitness.summary: How to avoid the most common beginner mistakes, where to start, and how to build a sustainable foundation in your first four weeks.
startingFitness.p1: The biggest mistake beginners make is doing too much, too soon. The enthusiasm of starting a new program often leads to training 6 days per week, maximum effort, and severe soreness — followed by burnout and quitting in week 3. The most effective first 4 weeks are often the most conservative ones.
startingFitness.p2: For the first month, focus on three things: learning the movement patterns (squat, hinge, push, pull), building the habit of showing up consistently, and figuring out your schedule — what days and times are you most likely to actually train.
startingFitness.p3: Start with compound movements that train multiple muscle groups simultaneously (squats, push-ups, lunges, planks). They give you the best return on time invested and improve functional movement quality.
startingFitness.p4: Expect to feel awkward for the first few weeks. Your nervous system is learning new movement patterns. The AI feedback will guide your technique so you do not develop bad habits during this critical learning phase. Trust the process — you will feel the difference within 4 weeks.

buildingHabit.title: How to Build a Training Habit That Actually Sticks
buildingHabit.summary: Why motivation is unreliable, how the habit loop works, and which strategies actually keep people training long-term.
buildingHabit.p1: Motivation is unreliable. It spikes when you first start and fades within weeks. Habits do not rely on motivation — they are automatic responses to cues. The science of habit formation shows that the cue → routine → reward loop is what makes behaviors stick long term.
buildingHabit.p2: Design your training environment to make the right choice the easy choice. Lay out your workout clothes the night before. Schedule training like a meeting — in your calendar, at a fixed time. Know exactly what workout you are doing before you start. The programs in this app remove that friction entirely.
buildingHabit.p3: Track your streak. The psychological power of 'I have trained 7 days in a row and I do not want to break that streak' is remarkably effective. It shifts motivation from external (results, which are slow) to internal (identity — 'I am someone who trains consistently'). Your streak is visible on the dashboard. Protect it.

squatVideo.title: Squat Technique: Full Visual Guide
squatVideo.summary: Joint-by-joint breakdown of the squat — foot position, knee tracking, hip depth, and spinal alignment — with common error demonstrations.
squatVideo.p1: This visual guide walks through every major checkpoint of the squat: foot width, toe angle, knee tracking, hip depth, and spinal alignment. Watch how joint angles change through each phase and where form most commonly breaks down.
squatVideo.p2: The AI module tracks the same landmarks in real time during your workout. Use this lesson as a visual reference, then practice with the camera active to receive live corrections on the same cues.
squatVideo.t1: Feet hip-to-shoulder-width apart, toes turned out 15–30°
squatVideo.t2: Push knees out over toes — don't let them cave inward
squatVideo.t3: Chest tall, spine neutral throughout the descent
squatVideo.t4: Aim for parallel thighs before increasing depth or load

pushupVideo.title: Perfect Push-Up Form: Visual Guide
pushupVideo.summary: Hand placement, elbow angle, body alignment, and the full progression ladder from wall push-ups to advanced variations.
pushupVideo.p1: Breakdown of push-up mechanics from hand placement through elbow angle, body alignment, and range of motion. Multiple angles show the difference between correct form and the most common errors.
pushupVideo.p2: Watch the full push-up progression ladder — from wall push-ups to decline variations — with clear visual cues at each level.
pushupVideo.t1: Hands just outside shoulder width, fingers forward or slightly out
pushupVideo.t2: Elbows at 45° from torso — not flared wide, not fully tucked
pushupVideo.t3: Rigid body from head to heels — brace core and glutes before each rep
pushupVideo.t4: Lower until chest is near the floor, fully extend at the top

plankVideo.title: Plank Mastery: Alignment & Progressions
plankVideo.summary: Shoulder stacking, hip alignment, active bracing, and progressions — from standard plank to advanced variations with full technique breakdown.
plankVideo.p1: The plank is often underestimated, but most people unknowingly break alignment at the hips, neck, or lower back. This video shows the correct position from multiple angles and how to cue your body into it.
plankVideo.p2: Learn the difference between a passive hold and an actively braced plank. Progression variations are covered for when you're ready to advance beyond standard duration.
plankVideo.t1: Shoulders directly over wrists or elbows — no forward drift
plankVideo.t2: Hips level with shoulders — squeeze glutes to prevent sagging
plankVideo.t3: Neutral neck — eyes at the floor, not craned forward
plankVideo.t4: Increase duration before moving to harder variations

lungeVideo.title: Lunge Mechanics: Forward, Reverse & Walking
lungeVideo.summary: Visual comparison of lunge variations with knee tracking, step length, torso alignment, and progression from beginner to advanced.
lungeVideo.p1: Forward, reverse, and walking lunge mechanics are broken down side by side, highlighting differences in knee stress and muscle emphasis. Foot placement, step length, and torso position are demonstrated from front and side angles.
lungeVideo.p2: The reverse lunge is the recommended starting point for beginners due to its lower impact on the front knee. This video walks through the progression from reverse to walking to weighted lunge.
lungeVideo.t1: Front knee tracks over second toe — never collapses inward
lungeVideo.t2: Step length: front shin vertical, back knee approaches the floor
lungeVideo.t3: Torso upright — don't lean forward as fatigue sets in
lungeVideo.t4: Master reverse lunge before progressing to forward and walking variations

progressiveOverloadVideo.title: Progressive Overload: How to Apply It in Practice
progressiveOverloadVideo.summary: The five ways to apply progressive overload beyond adding weight — with examples from AI Fitness Coach programs and how to read your training data.
progressiveOverloadVideo.p1: Progressive overload is the single principle behind all training adaptations — but it's widely misunderstood as only adding weight. This video covers the five ways to apply it, with practical examples.
progressiveOverloadVideo.p2: Learn how to read your training data to spot stagnation early, when to push load and when to deload, and how the technique score doubles as a measurable form of progressive overload.
progressiveOverloadVideo.t1: Add reps before adding weight: 3×8 → 3×12 → increase load → 3×8 again
progressiveOverloadVideo.t2: Technique score rising from 70% to 90% is measurable overload
progressiveOverloadVideo.t3: Log every session — you can't manage what you don't measure
progressiveOverloadVideo.t4: Increase load by no more than 5% per week to avoid regression

reminder.title: AI Fitness Coach 💪
reminder.body: Time to train! You haven't worked out in over 24 hours.
